metadata.summary: Prototyping is building interactive representations at the fidelity level required to answer a specific learning objective before committing to production development.

content.title: Prototyping
content.summary: Prototyping is the practice of building interactive representations of a product — from paper sketches to code — at the fidelity level required to answer a specific learning objective before committing to production development. Static designs cannot convey how interactions behave, which means usability issues remain hidden until expensive development is underway. Prototypes surface these issues early, align stakeholders on expected behavior, and provide a testable artifact for usability research — at a fraction of the cost of discovering the problem in production.

why.p: Static designs cannot convey how interactions behave, which means usability issues remain hidden until expensive development is underway. A wireframe shows what is on the screen; it does not show whether a dropdown feels responsive, whether animation is confusing, whether a multi-step form flow is intuitive, or whether users understand the connection between one screen and the next.

Prototypes surface these issues early, align stakeholders on expected behavior, and provide a testable artifact for usability research — at a fraction of the cost of discovering the problem in production. A low-fidelity paper prototype takes hours to build and can validate core interaction assumptions. A mid-fidelity clickable prototype takes days and reveals flow issues. A high-fidelity code prototype takes weeks and tests performance and edge cases. Matching fidelity to the question being asked is the key to effective prototyping.

list.item: Validating interaction flows before writing production code
Presenting design concepts to stakeholders who cannot read wireframes
Running usability tests when no real product exists yet
Deciding between two competing UX patterns before committing to development
Documenting expected behavior for handoff to engineers
When the feature scope is a single static content page with no interaction
When a fully working staging environment already exists and can be tested directly
When the only unknown is visual aesthetics — use static mockups instead
When the timeline is so compressed that prototype iteration would delay the actual build

section.name: fidelity-selection
section.p: Prototype fidelity — the level of detail and interactivity — must match the learning objective. Over-polishing wastes time; under-specifying produces feedback on limitations rather than design.

rule.statement: Match prototype fidelity to the learning objective: low (hours to build) for concept validation, medium for flow testing, high for realistic experience testing, code for technical feasibility.
rule.rationale: Over-polishing before testing wastes time on detail that findings will discard. Under-specifying with too-low fidelity produces feedback on prototype limitations, not the design itself.

rule.statement: Define one specific learning objective per prototype — prototypes that try to test everything teach nothing.
rule.rationale: Unfocused prototypes produce unfocused findings; testers cannot distinguish signal from noise when every screen is in scope.

rule.statement: Prototype the highest-risk interaction first, not the most visually interesting screen.
rule.rationale: Visual interest is a proxy for design excitement, not user confusion. Risk — measured by how wrong assumptions could be — is the correct prioritization criterion.

example.title: Paper prototype for concept validation
example.note: Draw screens on paper, cut out interactive elements, user points to indicate "clicks," swap paper to show next screen. Pros: fast (hours), cheap, anyone can do it, no tool skills required. Use for: concept testing, early flow validation, workshop ideation.

example.title: Clickable prototype in Figma for flow testing
example.note: Create screen designs, add hotspots, link screens, define transitions. Pros: quickly shareable, testable on real devices, no code required. Use for: flow testing, stakeholder alignment, remote usability studies.

example.title: Code prototype for technical feasibility
example.note: Self-contained interactive implementation covering a single flow end-to-end. Pros: tests real performance and loading states, most realistic fidelity. Use for: technical feasibility validation, edge case discovery, high-stakes interactions where Figma fidelity is insufficient.

section.name: content-quality
section.p: Content quality in prototypes affects the validity of testing findings. Lorem Ipsum hides real design problems.

rule.statement: Use real data samples (even anonymized) rather than Lorem Ipsum — fake data hides copy-length bugs and information density problems.
rule.rationale: A form that looks clean with "John Doe / 123 Main St" breaks with "Bartholomew Krishnamurthy / 4521 Northeast Industrial Boulevard Suite 220".

rule.statement: Document what the prototype intentionally does NOT cover at the top of the file — prevents misuse during stakeholder review and engineering handoff.
rule.rationale: Undocumented scope gaps cause stakeholders to file bugs against prototype limitations and engineers to treat incomplete flows as product decisions.

section.name: iteration-discipline
section.p: How to iterate on prototypes based on findings without losing previous work.

rule.statement: Version prototypes (v1, v2) and keep old versions accessible during iteration — do not overwrite previous versions.
rule.rationale: Design decisions often regress to earlier states when new constraints emerge; previous prototype versions are the fastest way to compare approaches without rebuilding.

rule.statement: After testing, write findings as discrete issues with severity ratings before sharing with engineers — raw notes get ignored.
rule.rationale: Unstructured testing notes require engineers to extract actionable items themselves; severity ratings enable prioritization without a follow-up meeting.

section.name: handoff-artifacts
section.p: Complete prototype handoff requires more than a link to the prototype file.

rule.statement: High-fidelity Figma prototypes require an explicit interaction spec document in addition to the prototype — engineers still discover interaction gaps during implementation from prototypes alone.
rule.rationale: Figma hotspot prototypes communicate "click here to go there" but not timing, animation curves, state transitions, or conditional behavior. The gap between prototype and implementation is always larger than it appears.

rule.statement: A complete prototype handoff includes: prototype link, screen specs (measurements), interaction specs (behavior), assets (icons, images), component library references, and explicit edge case documentation (error states, empty states).
rule.rationale: Engineers working from incomplete specifications spend time guessing rather than building.

antipattern.description: Prototype built to too high a fidelity before any user testing has been conducted.
antipattern.reason: High-fidelity prototypes take weeks to build. If the first user test reveals a fundamental navigation problem, the investment is discarded. Low-fidelity is appropriate until the structure is validated.

antipattern.description: Prototype with no defined learning objective — testing "the whole product."
antipattern.reason: Unfocused prototypes produce unfocused findings. Testers cannot distinguish signal from noise; the synthesis session produces a list of problems with no prioritization basis.

antipattern.description: Findings documented as raw session notes, not discrete prioritized issues.
antipattern.reason: Engineering teams cannot act on raw qualitative notes. Findings must be synthesized into discrete, severity-rated issues before sharing — otherwise they are ignored in favor of concrete backlog items.

antipattern.description: Prototype overwrites previous version — no version history maintained.
antipattern.reason: Design decisions frequently regress when new constraints emerge. Without previous versions accessible, teams rebuild earlier explorations from memory.

antipattern.description: High-fidelity Figma prototype treated as a complete specification for engineers.
antipattern.reason: Figma hotspots communicate navigation but not state transitions, animation timing, conditional behavior, or edge cases. An interaction spec document is always required alongside the prototype.

section.name: tool-selection
section.p: Different tools serve different fidelity levels and use cases.

rule.statement: Paper: earliest ideas, no tool skills needed. Figma: all-around choice, low to high fidelity. Framer: complex interactions, code-based. ProtoPie: advanced animations without code. Storybook: component-level interactive demos. Code (React): highest fidelity, technical validation.
rule.rationale: Tool selection depends on fidelity goal and team skills; no single tool is best for all prototypes.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: How subagents can assist with prototyping at various fidelity levels.

rule.statement: A Claude subagent can receive a user story and a list of screens from a design spec, then generate a clickable HTML/React prototype that covers the critical flow end-to-end.
rule.rationale: Prototype generation from a design spec is mechanical pattern application suitable for automation.

rule.statement: The agent should be constrained to a single flow at one fidelity level — attempting to automate a full high-fidelity prototype in one pass produces unmaintainable code.
rule.rationale: Scope constraint is essential for agent-generated code quality.

rule.statement: Human review is required after generation: the designer must walk through the prototype and confirm hotspots, transitions, and edge states before any user testing begins.
rule.rationale: Agent-generated prototypes are scaffolding, not finished artifacts; human review is essential quality gate.

rule.statement: Agents can analyze a Figma prototype link and extract: list of screens, clickable elements, transitions, and missing edge states (empty, error, loading).
rule.rationale: Automated analysis catches incomplete prototypes before testing.

antipattern.description: LLMs generating prototype code do not know the design system; inject component library docs or a token file into context first.
antipattern.reason: Agent-generated code uses arbitrary component APIs; providing design system context ensures consistency.

antipattern.description: Agents over-scope: constrain to a single named flow in the prompt or the output will be unmanageable.
antipattern.reason: Without explicit scope constraint, agents attempt to build the entire product, producing unmaintainable code.

antipattern.description: Figma API returns node IDs, not screen names — pre-map IDs to names before passing to an agent.
antipattern.reason: Agents cannot parse node IDs meaningfully; pre-mapping enables agent to reference screens by human-readable names.

antipattern.description: Generated React prototypes often use useState in ways that break multi-step flows; agent should be told to use a simple state machine or step counter.
antipattern.reason: Without explicit state management guidance, agents generate fragile state handling.

antipattern.description: Prototype quality review requires a human designer — do not let an agent declare the prototype "ready for testing".
antipattern.reason: Agents cannot assess design quality; human review is the only valid quality gate.

antipattern.description: When agents write session scripts (facilitator guides), they omit think-aloud instructions; always append the standard preamble manually.
antipattern.reason: Agents produce generic scripts without the human-centric facilitation guidance needed for valid testing.

rule.statement: Define one specific learning objective per prototype — prototypes that try to test everything teach nothing.
rule.rationale: Focus is the foundation of effective prototyping.

rule.statement: Prototype the highest-risk interaction first, not the most visually interesting screen.
rule.rationale: Risk is the correct prioritization criterion, not visual appeal.

rule.statement: Use real data samples (even anonymized) rather than "Lorem ipsum" — fake data hides copy-length bugs.
rule.rationale: Content reality affects design validity.

rule.statement: Version prototypes (v1, v2) and keep old versions accessible during iteration; do not overwrite.
rule.rationale: Version history enables comparison and regression decisions.

rule.statement: Document what the prototype intentionally does NOT cover at the top of the file — prevents misuse in handoff.
rule.rationale: Scope clarity prevents stakeholder confusion.

rule.statement: After testing, write findings as discrete issues with severity ratings before sharing with engineers; raw notes get ignored.
rule.rationale: Actionable findings are the only output that drives implementation.

rule.statement: High-fidelity Figma prototype requires an explicit interaction spec document in addition to the prototype.
rule.rationale: Prototypes communicate visual hierarchy; specs communicate behavior details.

template.purpose: Prototype plan: objectives, fidelity rationale, scope, user flows, interactive elements, and testing plan
Per-participant testing notes template: task success, observations, quotes, issues found, and recommendations

script.purpose: Bash script to export all Figma frames from a named page to PNG via the Figma REST API
